_about: A hand-curated seed corpus of deep-cut books: works of real merit that popularity engines routinely bury. Used to augment live Open Library results and as an offline fallback. Counts are deliberately conservative; the point is the pick, not the metrics.

books.title: The Invention of Morel
books.note: A fugitive on a deserted island discovers the machinery of eternity. Borges called it perfect.

books.title: The Peregrine
books.note: One man follows falcons across an Essex winter until the boundary between watcher and watched dissolves.

books.title: The Street of Crocodiles
books.note: A Polish shopkeeper's town mutates into myth. Schulz was shot in the street by a Gestapo officer in 1942; most of his work is lost.

books.title: The Tartar Steppe
books.note: An officer spends his whole life at a remote fort awaiting an enemy that may never come.

books.title: Nightwood
books.note: A fever-prose masterpiece of desire and ruin in interwar Paris. T. S. Eliot championed it; almost nobody reads it.

books.title: The Third Policeman
books.note: A murderer, a bicycle problem, and a hell shaped like rural Ireland. Rejected in his lifetime, published after his death.

books.title: A Voyage to Arcturus
books.note: A pilgrimage across an alien planet where every landscape is a moral argument. Sold 596 copies on release.

books.title: The Book of Disquiet
books.note: The diary of a Lisbon bookkeeper who never lived and never stopped noticing. Assembled from a trunk of unsorted scraps.

books.title: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge
books.note: A young poet in Paris learns to see. The first great novel of pure attention.

books.title: Life A User's Manual
books.note: A Paris apartment building rendered room by room as an infinite puzzle. Built on a mathematical scaffold you never see.

books.title: Nada
books.note: A teenager arrives in postwar Barcelona to a household slowly eating itself. Written by a 23-year-old.

books.title: The Opposing Shore
books.note: On a frontier frozen in a centuries-old cold war, a young officer is drawn toward the forbidden far shore.

books.title: Ferdydurke
books.note: A grown man is dragged back to school and infantilised in a farce about the tyranny of form.

books.title: Petersburg
books.note: A ticking bomb, a father, a son, and a city dissolving into shadow. Nabokov ranked it among the century's four greatest novels.

books.title: Concrete
books.note: A single unbroken paragraph of a man failing to begin the book he has meant to write for a decade. Savage and funny.

books.title: The Man Who Loved Children
books.note: The most exact and terrifying portrait of a marriage in the language. Went out of print for twenty years.

books.title: On Growth and Form
books.note: Why living things take the shapes they do, argued through geometry. A century of biologists have kept it on the desk.

books.title: The Immense Journey
books.note: An anthropologist writes about deep time the way poets write about love. The prose alone is worth it.

books.title: Tristes Tropiques
books.note: The great anthropologist's anti-travel book: 'I hate travelling and explorers,' it opens, then it breaks your heart.

books.title: The Poetics of Space
books.note: A philosopher on drawers, corners, nests, and attics — why intimate spaces shape the soul.

books.title: The Gift
books.note: The little book that revealed how gifts bind and obligate across every human culture. Foundational and short.

books.title: Hydriotaphia, Urn Burial
books.note: Prompted by some unearthed funeral urns, the finest prose meditation on oblivion in English.

books.title: The Anatomy of Melancholy
books.note: A 17th-century doctor's sprawling, digressive, inexhaustible encyclopedia of human sadness. You do not finish it; you live in it.

books.title: The Death of Virgil
books.note: The poet's last eighteen hours, told in vast unspooling sentences. One of the hardest, most beautiful books ever written.

books.title: The Other Side
books.note: An artist is invited to a dream-kingdom that is quietly rotting. Predates and outstrips much later weird fiction.

books.title: Malina
books.note: A woman split between two men and dissolving into fire and archive. Fierce, fractured, unforgettable.

books.title: The Doll
books.note: A merchant's hopeless love, and a whole vanished Warsaw around it. Poland's great 19th-century novel, barely read in English.

books.title: Roadside Picnic
books.note: Aliens visited, left junk, and never noticed us — like litter after a picnic. The source of the film 'Stalker'.

books.title: A Field Guide to Getting Lost
books.note: Essays braiding maps, blue distance, and the uses of being lost. On the newer side, but built to last.

books.title: The Rings of Saturn
books.note: A walk along the English coast that keeps falling through into silk-worms, herring, and the wreckage of history.

books.title: Arctic Dreams
books.note: The definitive book on how the far north acts on the human imagination. Patient as a glacier.

books.title: The Sleepwalkers
books.note: How Copernicus, Kepler, and Galileo stumbled backward into the modern cosmos. History of science as tragicomedy.

books.title: Pilgrim at Tinker Creek
books.note: A year of ferocious attention at a Virginia creek. Terror and grace in equal, unbearable measure.

books.title: The Spell of the Sensuous
books.note: How alphabets may have severed us from the living land, argued by a philosopher who was also a sleight-of-hand magician.

books.title: The Man Without Qualities
books.note: An unfinished masterpiece: a whole doomed empire thinking out loud on the eve of the First World War.

books.title: We
books.note: The dystopia that taught Orwell and Huxley how. Written first, banned longest, read least.

books.title: The Blue Flower
books.note: The young poet Novalis falls in love with a plain twelve-year-old girl. Somehow luminous, wise, and weightless.

books.title: Cosmicomics
books.note: Bedtime stories told by an immortal who was there before the moon left the earth. Playful cosmology at its finest.

books.title: Journey to the End of the Night
books.note: A furious, foul, hilarious tour through war, colony, and slum. Reinvented the sound of the modern sentence.
